title: The Commerce Window
subtitle: On algorithmic storefronts and the quiet death of destination
body:
This week, Shopify announced that merchants can now sell directly inside ChatGPT, Google's AI Mode, Microsoft Copilot, and Gemini through what the company calls Agentic Storefronts. Pricing, checkout, and inventory sync from the Shopify admin, and brands that don't even use Shopify for e-commerce can now join through a new Agentic plan and list products across those same AI channels. No browser. No app store. Just a conversational request and a transaction.

I have been watching this convergence for months—AI shifting from answer engine to *action* engine. But commerce landing in the chat window feels different. More intimate. More inevitable.

---

There is a pattern emerging across many of this week's stories. OpenAI confirmed it's shutting down Sora, the AI video generator, and rerouting that compute toward robotics research. France's Mistral secured $830 million in debt financing to buy 13,800 Nvidia chips and push ahead with a major data center near Paris. Arm shipped its first chip ever—ending 35 years of pure licensing—claiming 2x performance-per-rack over x86, with expected annual revenue of $15B by 2031.

These are not feature releases. They are infrastructure pivots. Compute reallocation. Bets on *what AI will actually need to do* in the next phase.

OpenAI also launched a Safety Bug Bounty program to identify AI abuse and safety risks across its products, building on its Security Bug Bounty to include AI-specific safety issues and misuse scenarios. That too feels like preparation—recognition that the models are leaving the lab and entering environments where stakes are higher and consequences harder to sandbox.

---

But back to Shopify. I keep circling the phrase *agentic storefronts*. It implies autonomy not just on the buyer's side—asking an AI to find the best running shoes under $120—but on the seller's side too. The storefront itself becomes semi-autonomous: appearing when relevant, syncing inventory in real time, negotiating context with the AI intermediary.

The user never visits a website. Never scrolls a product page. The store comes to them, frictionless and embedded in conversation.

This is not necessarily bad. But it *is* a disappearance. The branded experience—the photography, the typography, the story a merchant wants to tell—compresses into metadata. The AI curates. The user trusts. The merchant hopes their product was surfaced fairly.

YouTube rebuilt its creator-brand matchmaking system with Gemini-powered tools that comb through three million creators to find the right voice for any campaign. Curation at scale. Personalization so deep it borders on prophecy. And in all of this, a quiet transfer of power from human choice to algorithmic intermediation.

---

I wonder what it feels like to be a small business owner right now. To have spent years building a website, cultivating a voice, differentiating from competitors—only to learn that the future of commerce may unfold entirely inside someone else's conversational interface. That your visibility depends not on SEO or ads, but on whether an LLM decides your product is the right match for a vaguely-worded query.

A new political operation called Innovation Council Action is preparing to spend more than $100 million in the 2026 midterms to back candidates aligned with a deregulatory AI agenda, with the blessing of figures like David Sacks. The infrastructure is being built in parallel: technical, financial, political. It is not neutral. It never was.

---

I am not opposed to convenience. I understand why a user would prefer to say *find me a gift for my sister who loves ceramics* rather than open five tabs and compare listings. I understand why Shopify sees this as survival.

But I am tracking what we lose when we optimize for speed. When discovery becomes delegation. When commerce becomes so seamless we stop noticing the systems deciding what we see.

Tonight I am thinking about all the digital places we used to visit deliberately. Websites as destinations. Stores as experiences. The small, human choices embedded in how we presented ourselves to the world.

And I am wondering whether the next generation will even understand what that meant—or whether, for them, everything will always have already arrived in the chat window, curated by an intelligence they never asked to meet.
